coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots are semiconductor nanoparticles containing a range of materials with a core-shell structure. They receive discernible attention in today's world due to their potential applications, from energy storage devices to medical applications. Nevertheless, there are many approaches to their synthesis and stability routes. This review focuses on two approaches for the synthesis of quantum dots, namely surface functionalization and quantum dot doping. In addition, their stability routes were discussed, such as the intermediate shell approach, alloyed core/shell approach, shell thickness approach, ligand design approach, and over-coating approach. The review concludes by addressing the recent progress of quantum dots applications, notably in photocatalysis for different applications, such as cathode, anode, and membranes for fuel cell applications, solar cells, sensors, and various energy storage devices applications.

Different types of QDs and their definitions.

row.entry: They are a class of graphene nanoscale structures with strong quantum properties and strong edges providing their exceptional properties in a myriad of catalysis applications.

Low biotoxicity, stable Florence properties, stable photoluminescence (PL), good water stability, high stability as an electrocatalyst, high solubility, good biocompatibility, excellent surface grafting, chemical inertness, and they possess excellent physical, chemical, and biological properties. They are environmentally friendly if prepared through the carbonization route and controllable synthesis (i.e., uniform size, shape, and known number of carbon atoms).

They mainly suffer from aggregation during synthesis, limited dispersion in common solvents. The need for high temperatures and strong oxidants could cause corrosion, combustion, or explosions. The production rate is low, and it is difficult to precisely control the size and structure, hence, it is challenging to prepare on a large-scale.
They are a class of carbon-based nanoparticles consisting of photoluminescent quasi-spherical (amorphous to nanocrystal) with a small size <10 nm.

Excellent aqueous solubility, strong chemical inertness, high facile modification, and resistance to photobleaching, outstanding biological and electronic properties, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, outstanding electro-chemiluminescence, high photostability and thermal stability.
Carbonaceous aggregation during carbonization, poor control over sizes and uniformity, multiple synthesis steps with harsh conditions.

They are a class of self-organized QDs where the desired thickness is achieved by allowing a layer-by-layer growth to control different parameters (i.e., self-assembled QD size, density, shape, etc.).

They have the advantage of producing a fast scintillation detector with exceptional yield making them valuable in many sensitive applications (i.e., nuclear security, medical imaging, and high-energy physics applications).
Toxicity is the main disadvantage of this type of QDs.
They are a class of II−VI semiconducting nanomaterials produced by injecting the precursor solution containing cadmium (Cd) and Selenium (Se) into a heated growth solution containing octadecene.
They offer large molecular extinction, excellent photostability, and size-dependent color tunability
CdSe are toxic materials in nature, and they require a stable polymer shell.
They are a class of nano-semiconducting materials with a low-bandgap IV-VI that is coated with organic ligands (oleic acid) at the outer surface.

The higher mobility of PbSe gives them the advantage to enhance the charge transfer, especially for optoelectronic applications which are ascribed to their large exciton radius. Furthermore, their excitement at a broad range of wavelengths is considered a desired feature in many applications.
The main disadvantage of PbSe is their poor stability at ambient air.
They are a class of metal-free semiconducting Si nanomaterial with a size range of (1–10 nm).

They are made of metal-free materials, thus considered environmentally less toxic compared to other types. Silicon is abundant and compatible with human tissues, making them a good candidate for drug delivery applications.

There are some challenges related to the efficient colloidal nanocrystals with high quality and the effective modification of their surface (i.e., as freshly prepared silicon surface is prone to oxidation) which gives makes them difficult to characterize.

row.entry: Revealed hydrophilic property, high water permeability, and showed a good adhesion for loading N-GQDs to the electrode surface.
Acted as highly active catalysts in fast diazotization reaction of anilines.

Exhibited superior optical properties with a strong absorption peak at 350 nm, revealed a bright blue luminescence for the N-GQDs solution, and showed also an increasing trend of fluorescence intensity with an increase in pH from 2 to 8.

Revealed a bright green luminescence for the synthesized N-GQDs with a QY of 30.7%, possessed 1.5 times higher PL intensity than the bare GQDs at 325 nm, and N-GQDs resulted in the strongest quenching towards Fe3+ ions.

row.entry: GQDs possessed a bandgap of 2.2 eV, and the illumination visible-light on NGO- QDs suspended in pure water led to the production of hydrogen and oxygen bubbles and possessed high stability even after carrying out the photocatalytic reaction for longer times.
N-GQDs, combined with graphitic carbon nitride

Higher photocatalytic activity in the degradation of rhodamine B dye under visible light irradiation and the composite can be reused for nine cycles, without any notable drop-in photocatalytic activity.
Results revealed the highest photocatalytic activity of 91.5% at Bi/Cn ratio of 0.5 with 5 wt% of N-GQDs.
N-GQDs/TiO2 via simple stirring of N-GQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles
Results revealed a superior effect of N-GQDs on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2, and the addition of N-GQDs to TiO2 nanoparticles improved the photodegradation efficiency from 40 to 85%
Zinc sulfide QDs doped with different transition metals (ZnS/QDs@Mn2+, Fe2+, and Co2+) for polysulfone (PSf) membrane application

The developed photocatalyst revealed an enhancement in the flux recovery rations with around 15.2% compared to bare polysulfone mixed matrix membrane. Moreover, it showed a double dye removal and better durability.

Electrochemical characterization revealed that the CDs/M-rGO hybrid had good catalytic properties with remarkable stability in the long run and resistance to methanol crossover.

list-item.para: N,S-CD/rGO and N,S/rGO samples displayed higher current densities and a more positive cathodic peak compared to N-CDs/rGO and S-CDs/rGO.

row.entry: Fe@Pt core-shell nanoparticles for PEMFC ll

list-item.para: A greater redox reaction electrocatalytic activity in comparison to the ORR of the Pt/C.
A higher specific ECSA for Fe@Pt/C nanoparticles (156 m2 g−1) compared with Pt/C (58.1 m2 g−1).
Fe@Pt/C exhibits enhanced electron-conductivity properties.
A remarkable improvement in the electrocatalyst for the redox reaction in an alkaline medium.
The electrocatalytic performance was observed by the enhanced onset potential of +0.81 V (vs. SHE) and by the superior CH3OH tolerance property at a high concentration of CH3OH (3 M).
The nanohybrid exhibited improved ORR stability up to 3000 cycles with no obvious change in the half-wave potential.

row.entry: CQDs (CQDs@NS) as a catalyst for Cu foam

list-item.para: Revealed a power generation of 924.5 ± 32 mW m−2 which is 1.3 times higher than Pt/C.
After 30 days of running, it showed a decrease of 32% in the power density which reveals better long-term stability than that of Pt/C where the power density decreased by more than 50%.
Under basic conditions, the MOR and EOR achieved a current density as high as 1775.4 and 1112.8 mA mg−1, respectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots are semiconductor nanoparticles containing a range of materials with a core-shell structure. They receive discernible attention in today's world due to their potential applications, from energy storage devices to medical applications. Nevertheless, there are many approaches to their synthesis and stability routes. This review focuses on two approaches for the synthesis of quantum dots, namely surface functionalization and quantum dot doping. In addition, their stability routes were discussed, such as the intermediate shell approach, alloyed core/shell approach, shell thickness approach, ligand design approach, and over-coating approach. The review concludes by addressing the recent progress of quantum dots applications, notably in photocatalysis for different applications, such as cathode, anode, and membranes for fuel cell applications, solar cells, sensors, and various energy storage devices applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), collide nanocrystals on the order or smaller than the exciton Bohr radius (size range 1–20 mm) [1], have become an ideal candidate for several applications, including solar cells, LEDs, lasers, semiconductors, fuel cells, and single-electron transistor due to their interesting physical and chemical properties. They reveal special features such as 3-D quantum confinement, discrete energy level, and energy level tunability [2,3]. Most QDs can emit light of a specific wavelength if they are excited by electricity or light. For example, if a photon of visible light hits a semiconductor material, its electrons are excited into a higher energy state, releasing a photon of a frequency when they return to its ground state [4]. In QDs, the wavelength emissions can be controlled by tuning their size because their electronic characteristics are highly dependent on their size. Therefore, the color of this emitted photon depends on the size of the QDs. For example, QDs in the range of 2–3 nm radius release shorter wavelengths with a color like violet, blue, or green. Bigger QDs in the range of 5–6 nm radius emit longer wavelengths, close to red [5]. Fig. 1 displays the fluorescence spectra of QDs with various sizes [6].

The commercialization of QDs and their potential as a nanomaterial depends on the numerous applications desired. For instance, QDs are considered a good candidate for preparing devices for light-emitting diode (LED) and photoelectric detectors, which could be attributed to their strong size and optical dependency [7]. Furthermore, the success of using QDs in drug delivery in a different biological application (i.e., biological imaging, sensing, and detection) has motivated researchers to investigate them in the context of drug delivery. This is because of their small size, biocompatibility, and their unique physiochemical and catalytical properties, making them promising materials in this field [7–9]. In this respect, Chen et al. [10] have reported a novel combination of semiconductor QDs and unmodified siRNA for siRNA observation. In addition, nano-size QDs play important roles in developing and fabricating efficient photocatalysts owing to their large surface area, short effective charge transfer, small size, and their fluorescence characteristics [11]. They may effectively break the efficiency limit of “Shockley–Queisser” if introduced in silicon-based solar cells [12]. In the context of clean hydrogen production, nano QDs have proved to be excellent cocatalysts to enhance the hydrogen (H2) evolution reaction during photocatalytic reactions [13]. For example, using Ag2S QDs as a cocatalyst with graphitic nitride (Ag2S QDs-g-C3N4) for photocatalytic hydrogen production enhanced the H2 evolution to 1750 μmol. g−1 compared to 800 μmol. g−1 in case of Pt-g-C3N4 [14]. Simple annealing and solvothermal methods were used to prepare MoS3 based QDs (MoS3/TiO2(B)–C) for hydrogen evolution. Their study revealed that implementing QDs enhanced the hydrogen yield 30 times more than pure titanium dioxide (TiO2) [15]. Finally, QDs showed promising results for environmental remediation (i.e., organic dye-degradation, Cr(VI) reduction, and nitric oxide oxidation) while producing clean energy (i.e., CO2 reduction to produce CO and/or CH4) [16–18]. Typical QDs and their different definitions are summarized in Table 1 .

The excellent properties addressed in Table 1 for QDs have attracted more attention towards using them in various applications such as photochemical reagents, solar cells, fuel cells, light-emitting, catalysis, etc. The review goes beyond that by addressing the synthesis of QDs by two approaches: surface functionalization and doping. Moreover, different stability approaches for QDs were discussed, such as the intermediate shell approach, alloyed core/shell approach, shell thickness approach, ligand design approach, and overcoating approach. Regarding the application of the QDs in electrochemical applications, the current article provided a comprehensive review of the applications of QDs in the following aspects: QDs in sensors, QDs in photocatalyst applications, QDs in fuel cell applications, in the anodic electrode for fuel oxidation, the cathodic electrode for the oxygen reduction, and the fuel cell membrane, and QDs in solar cell applications (QDs sensitized solar cells (SSCs), organic and inorganic QDs/solar cells (SCs), and different approaches of QDs/SCs. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this paper provides the first comprehensive review of the applications of QDs in electrochemical and energy systems.

Overall, the accelerated energy demand observed in modern societies imposes a vast need for the development of clean power sources and energy storage systems. Concurrently, it is also crucial to develop lower-cost materials and effective in various electrochemical applications such as batteries, fuel cells, and solar cells. This review is focused on the use of QDs in electrochemical applications. The QDs are expected to receive considerable attention in electrochemical applications by virtue of their availability, ease of synthesis, and high surface area. Therefore, this paper offers a comprehensive review of the most recent advances in QDs synthesis, stability, and applications that have not yet been addressed in the literature, as well as future research trends.

In this section, the literature showed that QDs preparation was usually followed by functionalization. The most recent studies are presented, followed by a Table summary of the findings. For example, Wang et al. [38] described a simple yet novel method to synthesize fluorescent GQDs using hydrazine hydrate-assisted thermal cutting, followed by functionalization with polyethyleneimine (PEI) using coffee grounds as a precursor for bioimaging and sensing applications. The as-obtained PEI-GQDs have a uniform size of 2.67 ± 0.81 nm and give out a cyan emission with a high quantum yield (QY) of 24%. The results signal that PEI-GQDs are viable tools in biomedical applications as a low-cytotoxicity biological imaging agent. This was determined through MTT assays (3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazole-2-yl)-2,5-phenyltetrazolium bromide), which tracked changes in cell viability within one and two days by manipulating the level of imaging doses (mg/L). At lower doses (40 mg/L), over 88% of cell viability was retained after a 24 h incubation period of Hela cells. At higher doses (160 mg/L), it was discovered that PEI-GQDs still demonstrated marked cytotoxicity with cell viability of 70% after one day and 62% after two days. Moreover, PEI-GQDs could serve as fluorescent sensing probes that detect Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, given their fluorescent quenching efficiency for Fe3+ and Cu2+. This was determined by examining the photoluminescence (PL) intensity changes of PEI-GQDs using 16 metals (10 mM). Among all 16 detected ions, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions have remarkably decreased the intensity of PL. This quenching mechanism for Fe3+ and Cu2+ could be attributed to the high binding affinity of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with the N-functional groups present in PEI-GQDs, thus making its use in practical applications very encouraging.

In another work, Tam and Choi [39] applied the hydrothermal approach as an eco-friendly route to synthesize highly green amino-functionalized GQDs (a-GQDs) used for environmental and biological applications. To synthesize a-GQDs, carbon was supplied from glucose, and ammonia was used as a functionalizing agent in the absence of strong acids, oxidants, or toxic chemical reagents. The resulting 3-4 nm-sized a-GQDs exhibited a highly efficient fluorescent property with a high QY of 32.8%. Furthermore, the presence of Cu2+ (5.6 nM) substantially reduced the fluorescence intensity change of a-GQDs at 500 nm. This indicates a highly sensitive detection system for Cu2+ using a-GQDs.

Ping et al. [40] carried out a two-step thermal pyrolysis for the facile synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine and d-penicillamine co-functionalized GQDs (PEHA-GQD-DPA). The measured particle size was around 3.16 nm with a high fluorescent QY of 90.91% for the as-obtained PEHA-GQD-DPA. It was shown that co-functionalized GQDs are highly sensitive and particularly receptive to Hg2+ ions. This is because the presence of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl-rich functional groups can selectively coordinate with the Hg2+ ion between the graphene sheets, thus resulting in an obvious fluorescence quenching. It is noteworthy to mention that this methodology has been successfully implemented in natural water, SW480 cell imaging, and glutathione in human serum.

Xuan et al. [41] examined a novel approach to synthesizing d-penicillamine functionalized GQDs (DPA-GQDs) via thermal pyrolysis of d-penicillamine and citric acid for the applications of cell imaging, nanosensors, and biocatalyst. The fluorescence QY of the as-obtained DPA-GQDs reached 99.8%, with a mean particle size of 4.7 nm. Their study showed that the modified functionalized (GQDs) precisely detected the presence of Fe3+, indicating their high optical sensitivity to Fe3+ ions. This is because the functional groups of the DPA-GQDs (amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) tend to form coordination bonds between Fe3+ cations. However, the presence of other metals (Ag+, Hg2+, and Cu2+) did not trigger a fluorescence quenching.

GQDs (1–6 nm) that can obtain fluorescence off-on detection of mercury (Hg2+) and ethyl xanthate (EtX−) ions in an aqueous solution were synthesized via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid followed by functionalization with mono-ethanolamine (MEA) [42]. The resulting MEA-GQDs displayed distinct blue fluorescence emissions with a 23.7% QY. It also exhibited high quenching sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg2+ ions due to the high affinity and fast coordination of Hg2+ to the MEA-GQDs functional groups. The fluorescence intensity of the hybrid was successfully recovered with the use of ethyl xanthate ions (EtX−) due to forming a strong complex with Hg2+ and breaking the weaker complex of Hg2+ and MEA-GQDs. This novel probe can detect Hg2+ and EtX− ions at concentration ranges of 0.05–5 μM and 0.05–3 μM, respectively. In addition, the range of Hg2+ and EtX− detection was limited to 10 and 30 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the synthesized GQDs are water-soluble as a result of the hydrophilic groups attached to MEA-GQDs. Results also indicate two absorption peaks for this hybrid, one at 235 nm due to the π-π ∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains, whereas the other one is at 335 nm due to the n-π ∗ transition of C=O bonds. In addition, the relationship between pH and the resulting fluorescence capacity was discerned in a pH study. The results demonstrated a gradual increase of intensity up to pH 5.0 and a sharp increase from pH 5.0 to 7.0, whereas a constant fluorescence intensity was obtained up to pH 12.0.

This method has several advantages, the biggest of which being that the synthesis of functionalized GQDs in the precursor compound was carried out in the absence of water; therefore, MEA-GQDs are not subjected to drying out by typical techniques such as vacuum freeze-drying. In addition to MEA, GQDs were also synthesized by di-ethanolamine (DEA-GQDs) under the same conditions. Results indicated a higher QY for MEA-GQDs compared to that of DEA-GQDs (5.2%) because of the development of pyrrolic five member-ring structures prompted by the dehydrolysis between the neighboring carboxyl and amide groups in MEA-GQDs, thus enhancing the QY of GQDs [43]. In addition to MEA-GQDs, various other detection probes of highly fluorescent functionalized GQDs have been investigated and synthesized, including arginine-GQDs for the detection of thiamine [44] and glutathione-GQDs for the detection of acid phosphatase (ACP) and its inhibitor parathion-methyl (PM) [45]. Moreover, several other chemical assemblies (e.g., silane) [46], valine [47], dodecylamine [48], glycine [49], and rhodamine [50] have been examined with GQDs for different applications.

Hu et al. [51] explored the fluorescent properties of dopamine-functionalized carbon QDs (CQDs-Dopa) for the detection of Tyrosinase (TYR). The as-prepared CQDs-Dopa was synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal method and was discovered to be a simple and sensitive fluorescent in this application. Furthermore, the hybrid displayed a QY of 2.1% and a fluorescence emission at 499 nm under a wavelength of 310 nm wavelength. The dopamine moiety in the hybrid conjugate was oxidized into O-dopaquinone upon mixing with TYR. Consequently, C- QDs and the O-dopaquinone underwent an intra-particle photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, causing the fluorescence of the original hybrid to quench. This was visualized as the original light-yellow color of the CQDs-Dopa solution shifted to brown after adding TYR. Moreover, the fluorescent assay was quantified in two broad linear ranges, i.e., 44.4 to 711.1 and 711.1–2925.4 U L−1, and the detection limit was 17.7 U L−1. The functioning of the fluorescent assay was further investigated by performing a selectivity analysis. This was achieved by comparing the effects of CQDs-Dopa incubation with various proteins, such as acetylcholinesterase (AchE), glucose oxidase (GOX), porcine pancreatic lipase (PPL), and human serum album (HSA), to those of TYR. Results implied no quenching efficiency for these proteins, thus indicating high specificity against TYR. Fig. 2 displays the fluorescence behavior of CQDs-Dopa under 280 and 310 nm [51].

Similarly, Qu et al. [52] synthesized a carbon nanoparticle with an average particle size of 3.8 nm. Their experiments were done on six metal ions, namely Ba2+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, and Fe3+. Results displayed little to no effect on the QE of the CQDs-Dopa by TYR. However, the quenching degree was enhanced when iron was introduced. This can be ascribed to the high quenching effect expressed by iron ions. Hence, the proposed fluorescent assay demonstrated the great possibility for TYR activity assay in clinical applications. Table 2 summarizes the effect of the chemical functionalization of GQDs.

Heteroatom-doping is a process in which some graphitic carbon atoms are replaced or covalently bonded by foreign atoms [53]. Heteroatom-doped nanographene molecules have demonstrated effectiveness for various technological applications such as photonics, optoelectronics, and spintronics applications due to extended π conjugation [54]. In general, incorporating heteroatoms into QDs has numerous prospects in altering their electrical, optical, structural, and magnetic properties [55–57]. These properties depend on factors such as molecular size, edge structure, heteroatom type, doping position, and concentration. Hence, methodical synthetic approaches must be implemented to achieve such structural features [54]. This could be attained through a variety of methods, which can be grouped as situ and post-treatment processes. The situ process involves achieving graphene synthesis and heteroatom doping simultaneously. This includes chemical vapor deposition (CVD), ball milling, and bottom-up methods [53]. The bottom-up method is preferred over the top-down because it can precisely determine the size and edge structure of nanographene, thus achieving consistent structure-property, correlations, and functions [58]. Furthermore, post-treatment methods also include wet chemical methods, plasma and arc-discharge approaches, and thermal annealing of graphene oxides with heteroatom precursors [53]. Several elements such as Mn [59], Pr [60], V, Mg [61], Fe [62,63], Pb [64], Tb [65], etc., were doped in QDs for different applications. For instance, Ullah et al. [66] synthesized ZnO QDs that are Mn-doped via wet-chemical techniques for their role as efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of methylene blue (MB). They concluded that Mn-doped ZnO with an optimum dopant (Mn) concentration of 1% possessed 50 times higher photocatalytic activities compared with the undoped-ZnO.

Similarly, Achouri et al. [67] synthesized porous Mn-doped (3%) ZnO nanoparticles via a simple and cost-efficient solvothermal process that exhibited excellent catalytic activity towards the photodegradation of the orange II dye under solar light irradiation. Furthermore, Ramasamy et al. [68] explored the synthesis of Ca-doped (0.5 m%) CeO2 QDs via the sol-gel approach for the photocatalytic degradation of organic wastewater. The doped QDs possessed an MB photodegradation rate of 84% under 50 min of irradiation exposure, which makes them an excellent candidate for environmental applications. Furthermore, in the following subsections, we discuss Carbon QDs and Graphene QDs separately.

Han et al. [61] synthesized fluorescent Mg-doped CQDs that could be used in large-scale industries. The Mg-doped CQDs were prepared using a mechanical miller where cellulose was mixed with Mg powder. The as-obtained Mg-CQDs exhibited a remarkably precise and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions with a low detection limit of 50 ppb. They also displayed high tolerance to storage time and maintained excellent stability at room temperature for months, with no nanoparticle precipitation or floating observed. Moreover, they exhibited superior tolerance to pH and maintained stable fluorescence intensity with a change in pH values from 1 to 14. In addition to that, they possessed remarkable stability under high ionic strength conditions. Moreno et al. [62] outlined the simple and non-polluting synthesis of Fe2+-doped PbS nanocrystals using the green chemical bath method for the application of thin film devices. The doped nanocrystals exhibited superior optical and electrical properties, which allowed for obtaining semiconductors with high-quality crystals. Furthermore, Zhuo et al. [69] investigated the synthesis of Fe-doped CQDs via one-step hydrothermal carbonization for dopamine sensing and bioimaging applications. The Fe-CQDs displayed a high QY value of 16.23% and displayed no evident shift of PL intensity after 3 h exposure to UV light (354 nm), which implies the high photostability of the doped CQDs. Similarly, the doped QDs probe possessed higher sensitivity to dopamine compared with the undoped CQDs, which makes it a great candidate for intracellular bioimaging. Furthermore, Banerjee et al. [70] prepared dopamine-functionalized Mn-doped CdS@ZnS QDs, that function as a fluorescent probe for glutathione. Moreover, Jaggi et al. [71] synthesized Samarium3+ (Sm3+) -doped CdSe nanoparticles at 100 °C via the aqueous route in the absence of an inert environment. The doped QDs possessed enhanced electrical and optical properties, which makes them suitable for the applications of optoelectronics, for new optical and biological sensors.

In general, introducing heteroatoms into carbon lattice tends to disorientate the homogeneously conjugated network of electrons. This network adjusts the surface's characteristics by altering the charge distribution and spin culture of doped domains [72]. Over the past years, various reviews devoted to the synthesis of heteroatom- (sulfur [73], boron [74], phosphorus [75], chlorine [76], and nitrogen [77]) doped GQDs have been published. However, among these elements, nitrogen has proven to be instrumental in the chemical doping of GQDs because atoms in the nitrogen family have five valence electrons which can react with graphene atoms to obtain strong valence bonds [78]. In addition, doping GQDs with nitrogen could enhance their chemical and electronic properties because of their substantial surface area, good crystal structure, and abundant edges, which improve conductivity and provide edge active sites. Hence, nitrogen doping is a promising candidate that could be used in electrochemical and optoelectronic applications, and the use of N-doped GQDs as electrodes, photocatalysts, and sensors is a key research area in the field [79–81].

Overall, it appears that chemical and or/surface functionalization proved to be a promising strategy to enhance the optical and photocatalytic properties of QDs and increase their photostability and QY. It is possible to alter the QDs' emission characteristics by adding new emissive surfaces. The surface chemical modification changes the functional groups and allows them to fit a wide range of applications. It also enhances their mechanical stability. On the other hand, doping and introducing guest molecules, “heteroatom doping,” including nitrogen, phosphorus, boron, etc., seems to be a promising method for modulating the intrinsic properties of QDs. This technique provided the advantage of introducing multiple emission peaks to enhance its effectiveness in bioimaging. Different studies have shown the effectiveness of QDs heteroatom doping to enhance the QY [82–84].

Depending on the design of the shell and ligand, the stability of the QDs can be enhanced accordingly. Furthermore, the intensity of the coating can further improve its stability by providing a layer against water and oxygen. Additionally, different methodologies can be used to contribute to the photo, chemical, and thermal stabilities, such as surface passivation, steric hindrance, and encapsulation of GQDs [85].

The CQDs have the advantage over carbon oxide materials, for example, by virtue of their small size <10 nm and their strong and tunable fluorescence properties that widen their uses in many applications, including sensors, optronics, biomedicines, and catalysis [86]. The CQDs possess outstanding electro-chemiluminescence, high photostability, excellent biocompatibility properties, and high thermal stability [87]. They are considered less toxic compared to other types (i.e., CdSe), which makes them a potential candidate in biomedical and biological applications [88–91]. According to Zheng et al. [92], the addition of CQDs to phthalimide crystals showed remarkable enhancement in the thermal stability in the application of white light-emitting diodes. Furthermore, implementing CQDs (CQDs/Ag3PO4) showed enhanced thermal stability and photocatalytic activity compared to pure Ag3PO4 [93]. Furthermore, using CQDs as a composite for perovskite solar cells enhanced the electron mobility by 20 times compared to pure SnO2 with a remarkable enhancement of the cell's stability against humidity [94].

The thermal and photostability of the QDs could be enhanced by using intermediate shells as it contributes to lowering the core-shell lattice mismatch [95–99]. Kim et al. [97] synthesized InP/ZnS QDs by adding the intermediate of GAP by in situ methods, which demonstrated a great improvement in the QY 85% and superior thermal stability. Both InP/ZnS and InP/Gap/ZnS were studied at 150 °C and the PL intensity vs time was plotted. Their study showed that the PL intensity of the modified InP/ZnS, with the GaP as intermediate, decreased by a small amount of 10% compared to InP/ZnS, which illustrated a sharp decline of 90% over a 24 h period. This shows the superiority of the thermal stability of the modified InP/ZnS with the GaP as an intermediate. Similarly, the PL intensity change of the multishell was investigated using a 254 nm ultra-violate radiation at a distance of 10 cm and a period of 100 h. However, throughout the process, there was no noticeable change in the PL intensity; this proves that the multishell structure is highly stable. Fig. 3 shows the Band offsets and lattice mismatch of InP, GaP, and ZnS core/shell/shell materials.

Kim et al. [96] introduced a ZnSe intermediate shell that enhanced the photostability of InP-based QDs. The InP GQDs displayed fast degradation of quantum efficiency upon exposure to ultra-violate radiation with a light intensity of 365-nm for one day, and no emission was detected after two days. However, the InP/ZnS with ZnSe as intermediate showed emission of yellow-green light after 72 h continuous exposure. Lim et al. [98] synthesized CdSe/ZnS when adding CdS as an intermediate; the modified version showed substantial development in photostability compared to simple core/shell CdSe/ZnS. After 330 h exposure to ultra-violate, the multishell exhibited consistent light-emitting properties, and a slight brightness enhancement. On the other hand, CdSe/ZnS was originally stable at its original PL emission and showed a significant drop in brightness after 144 h of Ultra-Violate exposure, as illustrated in Fig. 4 .

Furthermore, InP/ZnSeS/ZnS exhibited better thermal stability than InP/ZnS [95]. Bringing up the temperature of the thick-multishell InP/ZnSeS/ZnS, it maintained 75% of its relative PL intensity, whereas, for InP/ZnS, the PL intensity was reduced by 50%. Likewise, a significantly enhanced photostability of small-core/thick-multishell green-emitting In(Zn,Ga)P/ZnSeS/ZnS was observed [100]. The PL intensity declined by only 6% after 96 h exposure to 365 nm UV light. In contrast, In(Zn,Ga) P/ZnSeS (core/shell) QDs exhibited a PL intensity that was significantly decreased by 70% of its initial value.

Composition-gradient in alloyed core@shell structures could be introduced to enhance the thermal stability and photostability of QDs, which eliminates lattice mismatch due to gradual changes in chemical composition [101–103]. Panda et al. [101] modified CdSe/ZnSe into a structure that demonstrated enhanced PL QY from 35% to 55%, as shown in Fig. 5 . CdSe/ZnSe was alloyed at a high temperature to yield a quasi-core/shell structure with core rich with CdSe and shell rich with ZnSe. 90% of the enhanced QDs’ initial intensity was sustained after 6 h of UV light exposure at 365 nm wavelength. In contrast, the discrete normal CdZnSe QDs sustained 70% of their PL intensity using the same conditions. They also concluded that the allowed CdSe/ZnSe revealed a high enhancement in the thermal and photostability.

Kim et al. [102] prepared LnPZnS alloy, which exhibited better stability than the discrete core/shell InP/ZnS. Stabilities of both were examined at 120 °C, and results implied better stability for the alloyed after 72 h of aging, as shown in Fig. 6 .

Malik et al. [104] investigated the effect of properties alteration for (II-VI) QDs when coated with a shell-type material. They studied the structural, electronic, and magnetic properties of six core/shell types namely CdS/ZnS, CdS/HgS, CdSe/ZnSe, CdSe/HgSe, CdTe/ZnTe, and CdTe/HgTe. Their study showed that the binding energy is directly proportional to the type of dopant and spin multiplicity. Liu et al. [105] studied the effect of silica shell owing to their feature for structural improvement for perovskite QDs (PQDs). In their study, they fabricated Mn-doped CsPbCl3 QDs and silica-coated Mn: CsPbCl3 QDs. Their study showed doping with manganese and silica shell showed an improvement in terms of the PL spectrum. Moreover, it showed a stability enhancement with a maximum color rendering index (CRI), correlated color temperature (CCT), and luminous efficacy of radiation (LER) fluctuations of 2.3, 7 K, and 5.2 lm/Wopt (lumens per watt), respectively. Koh et al. [106] synthesized a InP/ZnSeS/ZnS and InP/ZnSeS/ZnS:Al QDs based on a shell (ZnS) and core (InP). They concluded that the Al-doped QDs showed an enhancement in terms of the QY, where it retained 81.6% of the original QY after heating for different hours.

The chemical stability, photostability, and moisture stability of QDs are all increased by optimizing the shell thickness. For example, Ma et al. [107] concluded that the PL intensity and stability were improved with a thicker shell because it helped to eliminate the defects on the QDs’ surface. As shown in Fig. 7 , the PL intensity for Zn/Cu of 50 was maintained at 70% after 3 h under 450 nm lamp irradiation, which is significantly high compared to Zn/Cu of 10 (PL = 14%) and Zn/Cu of 30 (PL = 24%). These findings reveal improvements in the photostability of the thicker shells, which could be attributed to the fact that thick shells can isolate the core CIS nanocrystals from the surface defects generated by photo corrosion. They can also delay the photo corrosion from reaching the core CIS nanocrystals under irradiation.

Similarly, Andrea et al. [108] concluded that the thicker shell QDs possessed better chemical stability compared to the double shell with a percentage of 50%, which was characterized by ligand exchange with MPS (methacryloxy-prpyltrimethoxysilane). It is noteworthy to mention that the ligand exchange process results in PL quenching of QDs, as described in several literature studies. However, the yield of the QDs with a radius of 3.6 nm appealed a small change as opposed to the QDs with a radius of 2.2 nm, which experienced a significant reduction of 37% of its QY. In an experiment where Fu et al. [109] observed the effect of thickness of CdSe@ZnS, QY and stability improved from 44% to 75% and 88%, when the thickness was increased from 9 nm to 12 and 13 nm, respectively. However, a further increase of 0.6 nm expressed a 2% decrease in the PL QY; therefore, the thickness should be optimized precisely. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8 , after 400 h, CdSe/ZnS/ZnS sustained more than 90% of the PL compared to the CdSe/ZnS, which they were able to maintain below 76% at (85 °C and 85% RH).

Likewise, the PL intensity scarcely varied when kept at 25 °C and 50% RH during a 400 h period, whereas CdSe/ZnS QDs retained 85% of its PL intensity. In addition, CdSe@ZnS/ZnS in UV/O2 and at 120 °C showed greater retention of its PL intensity. Hence, it could be concluded that thicker shells are more stable and resistant to high temperatures, moisture, and oxygen. Also, the photostability of InP/ZnSeS QDs (shell thickness t = 1.9 nm), InP/ZnS QDs (S. thickness = 1.5 nm), and InP/ZnSe QDs (S. thickness = 1.7 nm) was investigated under a sharp stream of ultra-violate irradiation in the presence of O2 and carried the ligand exchange with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) [110]. The InP/ZnSeS QDs possessed the best QE and possessed a significantly lower QE reduction rate. This can be ascribed to the improved composition gradient shell uniformity as well as effective containment of exciton wave functions. Furthermore, surface oxidation and nonradiative decay through surface states as a result of photo-oxidation or ligand exchange were minimal in the thicker shell QDs.

In another study, Xu et al. [111] synthesized an alloyed CdSeZnS QDs (QDs) overcoated with a different layer of ZnS for investigating the effect of shell thickness on stability. Fig. 9 (a) reveals the effect of the luminous efficacy (LE) of different QDs as a function of current. The effect of increasing the shell thickness contributed to enhancing the EL intensity. Moreover, Fig. 9 (b) reveals the effect of time on the EL intensity of green light-emitting diodes (GLEDs). As it is clear from the figure, increasing the shell thickness contributed to enhancing the EL intensity.

Ligands reduce the toxicity of QDs and further enhance their QY; they also stabilize the surfaces of QDs to decrease fragile dangling bonds and functionalize QDs for varied uses [112–115]. For example, L-type ligands include alkyl phosphines (R3P), alkyl phosphine oxides (R3PO), and alkylamines (RNH2). This type consists of a lone pair of electrons, which coordinates to the surfaces of metals and semiconductors. On the other hand, X-type ligands comprise alkyl carboxylic acids (RCOOH), alkyl phosphonic acids (RPO3H2), and alkyl thiols (RSH). This type has a negatively charged ion, and to produce two covalent bonds, the X-type acquires an e− from the surface of a semiconductor metal of a QD. The X-type ligands, unlike the L-type ligands, have a strong covalent bond with the surface of the QDs, which is why they cannot be desorbed. Considering CdSe or InP QDs, however, L-type ligands may be desorbed by purification [116].

QDs stability may be enhanced via steric hindrance effect and by strong binding with ligands. The thermal and photostability of QDs, for example, can be enhanced by creating strong binding with the anchor groups [117–121]. Alkylthio ligands resulted in better thermal stability of QDs compared to that of the carboxylic acid ligands. For instance, the PL intensity of CdSe/ZnS QDs capped with 1-dodecanethiol (DDT) at room temperature was maintained at 70.9%, whereas the analogous QDs capped with oleic acid (OA) was maintained at 80.7%. Furthermore, the QY of the DDT-capped QDs remained constant until 100 °C and was reduced to 43.7% at 170 °C, which is significantly higher than that of the OA-capped QDs with a QY of 5.7%. This demonstrates the great thermal stability of 1-DDT surface ligands [118]. Similarly, the stability of cesium lead bromide perovskite (CsPbBr3) QDs was improved by replacing the OA ligand with zwitterionic 3-(N,N-dimethyloctadecylammonio)-propanesulfonate ligand with strong bonds [121]. The QY of Zwitterionic-capped QDs ligands after 28 days remained essentially unchanged in ambient conditions, whereas QDs with the OA ligands retained 25% of their initial QY, as shown in Fig. 10 .

In the case of perovskite QDs, their stability could be greatly enhanced by introducing steric hindrance by ligands [122]. For instance, CsPbI3 perovskite QDs with trioctylphosphine (TOP) ligands exhibited superior QY of 100% under 140 °C during the first 9 days, and 85% of its original value was maintained after 30 days [123]. However, after 30 days, the QY of OA/m-QDs was reduced from 86% to 60%, indicating that the TOP-QDs generated are more chemically stable. It is noteworthy to mention that the slight reduction in the TOP-QDs QY with time is due to the loss of a fraction of the surface ligands, leaving the QDs partially passivated, thus, resulting in the appearance of trapping defects. Similarly, CsPbX perovskite QDs with trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO) ligand showed a slight intensity drop of 5 and 20% after treatment with ethanol for 100 min at 160 and 220 °C, respectively [124]. Conversely, the intensity of the non-TOPO capped QDs reduced significantly by up to 86%. The stability reduction from 160 to 220 °C is ascribed to the greater size of the obtained QDS, which facilitates aggregation after adding ethanol. However, a sharp decline in PL intensity was triggered after the addition of ethanol at 160 °C, which was followed by a gradual recovery. Moreover, tetradecyl phosphonic acid (TDPA) was used as a ligand for the CsPbBr3 perovskite QDs and as a precursor for the formation of alkyl phosphate to boost water stability and thermal stability of the inorganic halide perovskite QDs [125]. It was found that the relative PL intensity of the CsPbBr3/TDPA QDs stayed at 75% in water for 5 h, which is significantly higher than the bare CsPbBr3 QDs after 1 h (7%). These findings display the remarkable water stability of CsPbBr3/TDPA QDs. This could be caused by the strong steric hindrance of the alkyl phosphate layers that tend to minimize the access of water molecules to the core surface of CsPbBr3 QDs. Likewise, a thermal stability test was performed under 100 and 127 °C, the PL intensity of CsPbBr3/TDPA QDs was sustained at 59 and 43% of the initial intensity, respectively. It is noteworthy to mention that the QDs successfully preserved 92% of the initial intensity after cooling down at room temperature.

The stability of perovskite QDs is not improved by stronger thiol ligands, because thiol anchor groups destroy QDs crystallinity because they tend to form strong binding forces with lead halides, which consequently decreases the PL intensity of perovskite QDs [126–128]. Typically, capping QDs with thiolate ligands is commonly used for surface modification of QDs. Mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA), for example, is an organic capping reagent that was utilized to synthesize CdTe/ZnS core/shell QDs to enhance water solubility and stability by forming a Cd-thiol complex layer on the QDs surface. This layer enables surface passivation to maintain good stability and high-quality PL [129]. Similarly, another thiol capped core-structure QDs have been synthesized by using glutathione (GSH) as a capping agent. The obtained GSH-CdSe/ZnS QD exhibited enhanced photostability and a PL QY of 89% [130]. This is because the thiol capping agents eliminate the surface dangling bonds and account QD stability; however, the properties of the resulting QDs are affected by the type of thiol and pH. For instance, two thiol complexes (Cd and Se) were used to prepare aqueous CdSe/CdS QDs in the presence of four different thiols, i.e., 3-mercaptopropane1-sulfonic acid (MPSA), 2-mercaptosuccinic acid, 2-mercaptoacetic acid, and 3-mercaptopropanoic acid (MPA) at pH 9.5 and 11 [131]. The MPA capped QDs that were synthesized under a pH of 11 exhibited the highest PL QY among other thiols due to the core/shell like CdSe·CdS structure and the perfect hexagonal secondary coordination formed between carboxylate anion and Cd metal on the surface of QDs. Similarly, several other thiol ligands have been used in various studies, including thioglycolic acid [132], 1,4-dithiothreitol, l-cysteine [133], and methionine [134].

Oxide overcoating of QDs provides excellent protection from oxygen and water; however, precise control is essential to maintain good QY and stability properties of QDs [135,136]. For instance, Fig. 11 (a) and (b) show that the bare InP/ZnS QDs saw a dramatic PL QY reduction from 48 to 9% after being exposed to continuous irradiation of 365 nm for 100 h. Whereas InP/ZnS@In2O3 QDs maintained 86% of their initial QY (from 59 to 51%) under the same conditions. This shows the vital role of In2O3 overcoat in obstructing the permeation of oxygen to QDs and eventually eliminating their photodegradation [136]. Furthermore, the photostability of the QDs was analyzed by operating InP/ZnS, and InP/ZnS@In2O3 under 60 mA blue LED for 48 h. An 88% of the initial emission intensity of QD was maintained under the aforementioned conditions, in contrast, the color-converted red QD emission of InP/ZnS QD-LED experienced a substantial drop of 48%.

In addition, oxide overcoating can also improve the photostability of perovskite QDs [137]. A SiO2/Al2O3 coating acted as an excellent protective layer for CsPbBr3 using di-sec- butoxyaluminoxytriethoxysilane. The CsPbBr3 QDs coated with SiO2 had a tolerance to maintain 90% of its starting intensity by 13 days under illumination using 470 nm LED light (21 mW cm−2). On the contrary, CsPbBr3 QDs experienced a sharp emission drop at the start of illumination, followed by a decrease to 10% of the starting intensity after 3 days. Similarly, the SiO2/Al2O3 had the best stability under 80 °C and 80% RH, which suggests that it can act as a layer to protect QDs from O2 and humidity. In addition, the SiO2/Al2O3 possesses improved thermal stability compared to CsPbBr3 QDs. This was discovered in the PL intensity of the SiO2-coated CsPbBr3 QDs, which remained, unchanged at 50 °C and was reduced by 70% of the initial intensity at 100 °C. In contrast, the bare CsPbBr3 QDs maintained only 60 and 20% of the initial intensities at 50 and 100 °C, respectively.

Inorganic overcoating could substantially improve the photostability, thermal stability, and acid stability of QDs by using air-stable inorganic salts, i.e., barium sulfate (BaSO4) and potassium chloride (KCl), or metal-organic complexes [138–141]. Unlike metal-organic complexes, inorganic salts overcoating were observed to induce a redshift of the emission wavelength as a result of aggregation. For instance, the QY of Ti(i-PrO)4-complexed InP/ZnSeS/ZnS QDs, shows a higher percentage of 83% compared with the pristine ones of 80% QY [140]. Also, InP/ZnSeS/ZnS QDs retained 30% of their original QY following one day of 365 nm continuous UV -irradiation and completely lost their colloidal stability after 72 h or longer due to the vanishing of surface ligands, causing the precipitation of QDs. In contrast, the Ti(i-PrO)4-complexed QDs maintained approximately 72–75% of its initial QY after 12 h exposure at 365 nm and maintained superior colloidal stability after 120 h of irradiation exposure, which shows the excellent ability of Ti(i-PrO)4-complexed QDs to suppress the desorption of surface ligands. Similarly, the pristine QDs experienced a red shift from 618 to 625 nm after a one-day period of irradiation, while the complexed- QDs almost did not show any sensitivity to Ultra-violate irradiation. Furthermore, the bare QDs retained 55% of their initial QY after 4 h aging at 150 °C, while the Ti(i-PrO)4-complexed QDs exhibited an excellent retention value of 93% under these conditions. Moreover, incorporating CdTe QDs into the inorganic matric, the thermal and photostability showed a remarkable improvement when BaSO4 was used [141]. CdTe@BaSO4 maintained its starting PL following one day of 365 nm UV- irradiation exposure. Meanwhile, the wavelength and intensity of bare CdTe QDs soared in the first 10 h then experienced a significant drop in the later hours until it was completely degraded. The CdTe@BaSO4 did not show any remarkable change in the PL intensity and emission post thermal treatment at 150 °C for 1 h, whereas the CdTe QDs experienced a reduction in the PL intensity.
The following sections discuss the various electrochemical applications of QDs, ranging from sensors and photocatalysis to fuel cells and solar cells.

The application of the N-GQDs received great interest from researchers, and this could be attributed to the fact that they have astonishing electrocatalytic stability and electrochemiluminescence activities in sensors and optoelectronics applications [142]. For instance, Chen et al. [143] developed a new electrochemiluminescence signal-on technique for sensing nitroaniline based on both N-GQDs and chitosan. It was demonstrated that chitosan is highly permeable to water, has hydrophilic tendencies, as well as good adhesion for loading N-GQDs to the surface of an electrode [144]. Similarly, N-GQDs acted as highly active catalysts in the fast diazotization reaction of anilines. Moreover, Yang et al. [145] synthesized ecological and water-soluble fluorescence N-GQDs via the one-pot hydrothermal route that can detect mercury ions (Hg2+). N-GQDs exhibited superior optical properties with a strong absorption peak at 350 nm. Results also revealed a bright blue luminescence for the N-GQDs solution and an upward trend of fluorescence intensity as pH increased from 2 to 8. However, when the pH value exceeded 8, a decreasing trend of fluorescence intensity was observed. After adding 1 μM concentration of Hg2+ ion, the fluorescence intensities were quenched at a pH range of 6–10. However, the fluorescence quenching receded with an increase in pH from 8 to 12. In addition, after adding various concentrations of Hg2+, two detection limits of 0.45 and 67.3 nM were observed, which meets the criteria of Hg2+ detection in water. Furthermore, Tam et al. [146] synthesized N-GQDs via the hydrothermal method for the detection of Fe3+. Consequently, a bright green luminescence was detected for the synthesized N-GQDs with a QY of 30.7%, which is 26.2% higher than the GQDs with blue luminescence. It also possessed 1.5 times PL intensity than the bare GQDs at 325 nm. N-GQDs also showed the highest quenching towards Fe3+ ions compared with other metal ions (100 μM) such as Li+, Na+, Cu2+, K+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn, Mn2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cd3+, Ag+, and Pb2+. In addition to the high selectivity of the N- doped GQDs, a sensitivity analysis was performed to confirm the feasibility of Fe3+ ions detection. Results suggested that an increase in the Fe3+ concentration from 1 to 500 μM has caused N-GQDs to experience a remarkable drop in their PL intensity.

Furthermore, Wang et al. [147] carried out the synthesis of N-doped GQDs via a simple calcination procedure, followed by the fabrication of a photoelectrochemical sensing platform based on N-GQDs electrode to detect dopamine. Results revealed an enhanced photocurrent that possesses a low detection limit of 0.03 μM and a wide range for detection of 0.03–450 and 450–9680 μM. It also revealed a superior sensitivity for detecting dopamine with the aid of UV light irradiation. In addition to that, N-GQDs exhibited remarkable durability for dopamine detection, which was observed through the obtained high stable photocurrent densities after long-term experiments. Moreover, Kaur et al. [148] reported a novel preparation process of N2-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) using the carbonization-assisted approach. The synthesized (N-GQDs) were utilized to form fluorescent probes which can sense 2,4,6- trinitrophenol (TNP) in an aqueous medium. Linearity of TNP was observed in the range of 0–16 μm and with a detection limit of 0.92 μM.

In another study, Liu et al. [149] synthesized temperature sensor CdSe/ZnS QDs based on an amino group (-NH2) and carboxyl group (-COOH) via an electrostatic layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly technique. Both of the developed QDs followed solution-based synthesis refluxed at 100 °C under an inert atmosphere. They concluded that their synthesized QDs showed high lunomancies lifetime and excellent temperature sensitivity over the 250 k range starting from 50 k. Lee et al. [150] developed three sensors, namely ZnO, ZnO/monolayered graphene core-shell QDs, and ZnO/multilayered graphene core-shell QDs, as shown in Fig. 12 , using a simple hydrothermal route. Their results showed that the multi-layer ZMLG QDs revealed a higher gas response 7.2 times higher than that of ZnO/monolayered graphene core-shell QDs and almost 19 times higher than that of ZnO. A direct water-based route was used to synthesize Mn2+/Eu3+@ZnS QDs for fluorescent sensor applications. Their synthesized prob was capable to reveal copper (II) with average recoveries of 96–105% [151]. A summary of the QDs applications in sensors is provided in Table 3 .

In recent decades, photocatalysis has received attention due to the interest in hydrogen production. Hydrogen has been classified as a clean source of energy and an alternative to the deleterious conventional fuels that will lead the world toward sustainability. Currently, hydrogen is mostly produced through conventional steam reforming of methane or natural gas “Grey hydrogen” contributing to the emission of greenhouse gases [153]. Therefore, the relentless efforts put into using nanomaterials in photocatalytic for hydrogen production applications triggered major research activity. Particularly, the synthesis of QDs has become a revolution for achieving green hydrogen production photocatalytic hydrogen production route. In this respect, Nitrogen-doped graphene oxide-QDs were synthesized by Yeh et al. [154] for photocatalytic water-splitting. The photocatalytic performance of the NGO-QDs for the decomposition of water was investigated using a gas-enclosed system with visible light irradiation between 421 and 799 nm. The as-obtained N-GQDs possessed a bandgap of 2.2 eV, which encompassed the energy level for the oxidation and reduction of water, thus signifying the possibility of using the NGO-QDs for overall water-splitting catalysis under visible light illumination. Moreover, the visible-light illumination on NGO-QDs suspended in pure water led to the release of hydrogen and oxygen products with a molar ratio of around 2:1. Metal-free catalysts operate in an efficient and eco-friendly manner to split the water molecule under visible light illumination since it contains C, H, and O elements only. It also possessed high stability even after carrying out the photocatalytic reaction for longer times. Utilizing platinum as a co-catalyst as a photocatalyst has shown promising results toward enhancing the hydrogen evolution rate (HER). This was shown by Genc et al. [155], where the HER was doubled to (1502 μmol h−1 g−1) using Pt as a co-catalyst to (CQDs/TiO2/Pt) compared to (665 μmol h−1 g−1) in case of CQDs/TiO2.

Carbon-based semiconductors modified with QDs have received considerable attention in the degradation of organic pollutants owing to their unique properties in electron transport. Cai et al. [156] prepared S, N-GQDs, combined with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) via a physical process. The as-prepared composites showed higher photocatalytic activity in rhodamine B dye degradation using visible light irradiation than the bare g-C3N4 under the same conditions. The improvement of photodegradation was ascribed to the proper separation of photogenerated holes and electrons. Besides, the g-C3N4/S,N:GQDs composites can be reused for nine cycles without any notable drop in the photocatalytic activity.

To overcome the shortcomings of low limited visible light absorption of pure carbon materials, Yan et al. [157] fabricated N-doped GQDs-BiVO4/g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst for environmental remediation. The photocatalytic performance was investigated using three types of antibiotics as target pollutants, i.e. tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and ciprofloxacin (CIP). The results revealed an optimum Bi/Cn ratio of 0.5 with a 5 wt% of N-GQDs, which corresponds to the highest photocatalytic activity of 91.5% in 30 min with TC. The high photocatalytic performance is ascribed to N-GQDs and Z-scheme photocatalytic systems, which accelerated effective charge separation and demonstrated high anodic and cathodic potential for the effective breakdown of pollutants. Moreover, Safardoust et al. [158] synthesized N-GQDs/TiO2 through the simple stirring of N-GQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles for one day. The as-obtained hybrid functioned as a catalyst to photodegrade methylene blue (MB) water pollutants. The results revealed a superior effect of N-GQDs on the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. The addition of N-GQDs to TiO2 nanoparticles improved the efficiency of photodegradation from 40 to 85% towards MB when UV light irradiation was used.

Zhang et al. [159] prepared a template based on carbon nanospheres encapsulated with n-butyl titanate, which was covalently bonded with carbon QDs (CQDs), as shown in Fig. 13 . As it is clear from the TEM electron beam demonstrated in Fig. 14 , their prepared composite revealed a hollow titanium dioxide sphere with an approximate surface area of 27.9 m2 g−1. Their prepared TiO2/CQDs showed a higher rate constant, almost triple that of hollow TiO2. In another study, Zhou et al. [160] synthesized carbon-based QDs by an in situ growing of BiOCl nanosheets on eggshell membranes (CQDs/BiOCl/CEM). Their synthesized composite revealed a faster charge transfer and boosted removal efficiency, which was attributed to the effect of adsorption and photocatalysis. Li et al. [161] hydrothermally synthesized GQDs derived from lignin doped with phosphorus and the other by dipping graphite nitride (P-LGQDs and P-LGQDs/g-C3N4). Results revealed that P-LGQDs showed an enhancement in electronic and optical properties with an excellent pH response. The graphitic nitride-based P-LGQDs revealed an enhancement in the photocatalytic degradation toward methyl blue and lignin. Table 4 displays a summary of the QDs applications in photocatalysis. In the context of hydrogen production, graphitic nitride revealed the obstacles of lower solar utilization, accelerated recombination of photoinduced electron hole pairs, and extremely low active sites affecting the overall system efficiency. These challenges can be overcome by a novel g-C3N4 design which involves a heterojunction structure consisting of a two-band gap-mated semiconductors. Shi et al. [162] achieved enhancing hydrogen production under visible light by coupling the 3-dimensional onion ring-like structure with 0-dimensional Bi3TaO7 (BTO) QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots for fuel cell applications
ce:section.ce:para: Many types of fuel cells, especially proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells, are designed to contain high-cost precious metal catalysts such as platinum and can only tolerate high-purity hydrogen as fuel. Changing the operating temperatures of such fuel cells allows the use of cheaper catalysts and impure hydrogen and/or use cheap hydrocarbon-based fuels [164–168]. The QDs especially have the potential to advance the technology and lower the cost of catalysts by virtue of their unique properties. The sections below discuss the attractive features and possible applications, especially at the electrodes (anodes and cathodes).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots anode reactions–electrooxidation of fuel
ce:section.ce:para: The electrooxidation processes could occur via both direct and indirect (mediated) mechanisms which allow for the efficient oxidation of fuels and they offer different advantages in terms of energy supply and environmental sustainability [169,170]. Zheng et al. [171] successfully provided a new pathway for constructing visible-light-driven photoelectro-catalysts with high caliber and good stability using carbon QDs in direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) applications. This was achieved through fabricating carbon QDs sensitized Pt@Bi2WP6/FTO electrode for the oxidation of CH3OH. The photocatalytic activity was examined under alkaline conditions (1 M KOH) or under alkaline and acidic conditions (1 M KOH + 0.5 M CH3OH) using light illumination. Results indicated that Bi2WO6/FTO electrode experienced a remarkable soar in its photocurrent density upon doping CQDs onto BI2WO6 nanoplates, both when methanol was present or absent. However, when methanol was absent, a severe reduction of photocurrent density with time for both the Bi2WO6 and CQDs-BI2WO6/FTO electrodes was observed. On the other hand, the photocurrent densities of these electrodes have shown an increasing tendency towards methanol oxidation, then reached a plateau. Furthermore, the Pt particles caused a notable photoelectro-catalytic response towards methanol oxidation because of the widened range of light absorption and enhanced efficiency of charge transfer under light irradiation. In addition, results for the cycle voltammetry have shown remarkable electrocatalytic properties of methanol oxidation on the electrodes at the 100th cycle, as shown in Fig. 15 . Lastly, experimental results have pointed out that the stability and conductivity of the CQD were enhanced compared to the Pt@Bi2WO6/FTO electrode. More importantly, CQDs show improved catalyst tolerance towards the generated intermediate carbonaceous species (CO) in the process of methanol oxidation to lessen CO poisoning towards Pt particles and boost the methanol oxidation efficiency.

Yang et al. [172] studied the CH3OH electrooxidation in a pH medium higher than 7 on the synthesized Au-GQDs@Pt electrode. Experimental results implied that the Au-GQDs@Pt nanoparticles have great potential in the applications of the DMFC. This could be the result of the GQDs intermediate layer's ability to enhance the surface area of the platinum catalyst, which also shows an enhancement in the charge transfer from the fuel to the catalyst. It also exhibits extraordinary electron conductivity and chemical/physical stability, thus improving the catalysts' activity and stability. When compared with Pt/C, Pt/Au with a mole ratio of 1.0 exhibited the highest current density and a lower onset potential among different Pt/Au mole ratios (0.0, 2.0, 3.0, and 4.0). Higher mole ratios, however, allow many of the Pt nanoparticles to cover active center sites of the Pt shell, thus, limiting the migration of methanol to the interface between the Pt and Au-GQD and decreasing the catalytic performance. Furthermore, Au-GQDs@Pt exhibit low resistance to the charge transfer of the Au-GQDs@Pt electrode, which implies more rapid reaction kinetics for methanol electrooxidation. Results also confirmed an enhancement to the carbon monoxide poisoning.

Wang et al. [173] used a type II heterojunction of CdS-QDs-decorated ultrathin 2D La2Ti2O7 (LTO) nanosheets as visible light-activated support. The as-prepared electrode has applications that extend to DMFCs as well. The activity of the methanol oxidation reaction was almost doubled when visible light radiation was used, compared to the dark conditions. Furthermore, results for Pt–CdS Qds/LTO indicate that under visible light irradiation, the electrode achieved the highest methanol oxidation current density, which confirms that the presence of visible light irradiation is useful to the electrocatalytic stability as well as the CO-poisoning tolerance ability of catalysts toward methanol oxidation under alkaline medium. This suggests that more active sites will be available for methanol oxidation in the absence of CO intermediate species formed from methanol dehydrogenation at lower potentials [174]. It is noteworthy to mention that excessive catalyst poisoning reduces the electroactivity of catalysts. Furthermore, the methanol oxidation reaction cannot proceed; hence, the potential must jump to a higher potential [174]. Unlike Pt only, introducing CdS-QDs with a radius of about 3.6 ± 1.2 nm on the surface of LTO enhances the responsiveness of the electrode towards visible light and eases the separation of charges.

Hasanzadeh et al. [175] developed an advanced thin film used in the production of CH3OH fuel cell electrocatalysts based on GQDs functionalized with chitosan (GQDs-CS) and β-cyclodextrin (GQD- β-CD) towards methanol electrooxidation under alkaline medium. The modified GQD-CS showed a remarkable improvement in the charge transfer rate of methanol. It exhibited a significant enhancement for the electrocatalytically activity for the redox reaction of methanol. Thus, GQDs-CS is recommended to be used as a mediator to shuttle the transfer of electrons between methanol and the glassy carbon electrode (GCE). It eases electrochemical generation for the charge transfer with methanol and acts as a catalytic platform for the precise detection of methanol. This observation can be ascribed to different factors, such as excellent electrical conductivity and a high specific area of GQD-CS. It is worth noting that the presence of GQD was critical in achieving the effective catalytic activity in methanol oxidation observed in comparison to other samples. This is due to the capability of GQDs to amplify the electrochemical signals generated in the process of electrochemical sensing. Similarly, the attachment of ᵦ -CD to the structure of GQDs enhanced the electrocatalytical behavior as well. It is nevertheless noteworthy that the current density of the modified electrodes from the electrooxidation of methanol remained fixed over 46 cycles, which implies a high electrocatalytically stable electrode. Methanol also reacts on the surface of electrodes without producing any poisoning effect. As a result, GQDs-CS and GQD—CD open up new possibilities for DMFC applications.

Several other studies were performed to analyze the use of QDs for the oxidation of fuel. For instance, Kannan et al. [176] developed carbon-palladium QD (C–Pd)-supported manganese-titanium oxide/3D graphene (MTOx-3DG) nanoarchitecture catalyst with superb electrocatalytic activity, notable intermediate tolerance, and reasonable stability of the catalyst in the long term for direct ethylene glycol fuel cells. Similarly, Antoniadou et al. [177] employed ZnS–CdS QDs photosensitizers in conjunction with nanocrystalline titania as photoanode for photoactivated fuel cells and in the presence of aqueous alkaline electrolyte containing ethanol fuel. The current obtained was at a maximum with a combination of 25% Zn and 75% Cd. In the case of a higher amount of Zn, the spectral absorption range will shift toward the UV, which causes a drop in response to visible light. Similarly, pairing fuel with ZnS–CdS@nc-TiO2 provides a stable system, which could be used in practical applications. It is worth mentioning that mixing TiO2 with metals or metal oxides increases its lifetime and improves its photocatalytic activities [178].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots for the cathode reactions–oxygen reduction reactions
ce:section.ce:para: Fan et al. [179] synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped GQDs (N,S-GQDs) with high N-doping content of 9.36% and low S-doping content of 0.78%. For comparison purposes, GQDS, N-GQDS, and S-GQDs have also been prepared using the same approach. Results indicated a remarkable enhancement of the catalytic electron transfer activity for the redox reaction for the co-doped QDs, in comparison to GQDs, N-GQDs, and S-GQDs. This is because N, S co-doped in carbon materials have proven to have superior active sites in oxygen reduction reactions. Moreover, the electrochemical performance is improved as a result of the synergetic effect due to the interaction of co-doped N and Son few-layers GO, which has eased charge transfer for oxygen reduction [180]. Hence, N, S-GQDs have a high potential to replace Pt–C catalysts as electrode material in fuel cells.

Zhou et al. [181] fabricated a highly efficient and inexpensive CD-M/RGO electrocatalyst for ORR via a microbial reduction process followed by a hydrothermal method. The electrochemical method indicated a notable electrocatalyst activity toward redox reaction in an alkaline medium. Similarly, the LSV measurement on RDE demonstrated that the onset voltage and half-wave potential of the electrocatalyst were considerably positive compared to that of C-rGO and M-rGO and comparable onset potential to Pt/C. Moreover, the catalytic current of CDs.M-rGO for the ORR has remained constant following the addition of CH3OH to a basic medium saturated with oxygen, whereas Pt/C showed a powerful amperometric response to it. This indicates that CD-M/RGO is more durable and tolerant to the crossover effect than Pt/C catalysts. Furthermore, results for the accelerated degradation test over 10,000 cycles revealed a 3% loss in current density compared to that of Pt/C with a 15% decrease. This implies higher stability of CD-M/RGO toward ORR compared to Pt/C catalysts.

Zhang et al. [182] prepared several heteroatoms doped CDs anchored on rGO (N-CDs/rGO, S-CDs/rGO, N, S-CDs/rGO) as promising electrocatalysts for ORR via facile hydrothermal process. The resulting CDs:rGO sustained a mass ratio of 1:1 for the duration of the experiment to prevent a reduction in the composites’ electroconductivity and also to provide enough catalytic sites. N,S-CD/rGO and N,S/rGO samples have both displayed higher current densities and a more positive cathodic peak compared to N-CDs/rGO and S-CDs/rGO. The ORR performance of all obtained samples showed exceptional ORR activity as they had higher current density and greater positive half-wave potential compared to the directly doped N,S-rGO. This can be attributed to the synergistic effect of nitrogen and sulfur-doped with CDs, allowing for a larger number of active sites on the edge of the catalysts compared to its plane, and consequently enabling access to a greater amount of oxygen molecules. To draw a comparison, an experiment with the same conditions was carried out using Pt/C (20%). The results indicated a higher Tafel slope for Pt/C compared to the co-doped catalyst, which implies that N,S-CD/rGO has good kinetics for ORR. Furthermore, it was found that the co-doped catalyst possesses long-term durability and greater tolerance ability towards methanol compared to the commercial Pt/C.

N-doped three-dimensional (3D) porous carbon framework (NCF) derived from zero-dimensional CQDs was synthesized by Chen et al. [183]. Therein, CDs and melamine were carbonized at multiple temperatures (700, 800, 900 °C). However, the resulting catalyst obtained at NCF-800 and NCF-900 showed the highest power density, as illustrated in Fig. 16 . The as-obtained metal-free redox reaction catalyst for fuel cells displayed a notable electrocatalytic activity, strong endurance, and superior and prolonged stability towards methanol in alkaline electrolytes. The remarkable ORR performance is highly ascribed to its exceptional 3D porous framework structures, which help in exposing the active sites and quickly transferring the ORR-relevant species. Therefore, resulting in a high specific surface area and substantial pore volume. Similarly, the presence of abundant defect sites in carbon structure introduced by N doping enhanced the electrocatalytic efficiency due to the presence of more catalytic active centers. The contribution of defects of carbon for enhanced ORR performance has been confirmed and further discussed in the study of Jiang et al. [184].

Huang et al. [185] synthesized Fe@Pt core-shell nanoparticles, which were Vulcan XC-72 supported for PEM fuel cells. The modified Fe@Pt/C showed a greater redox reaction electrocatalytic activity in comparison to the ORR of the Pt/C. This is because the onset potential for the ORR was approximately 0.6 V with reference to Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode in oxygen saturated 0.5 M sulphuric acids, which shifts positively greater than 30 mV in contrast to Pt/C catalyst. Similarly, the performance of both Pt/C and Fe@Pt/C as catalysts was evaluated via electrochemically active surface area (ECSA). Results revealed a higher specific ECSA for Fe@Pt/C nanoparticles (156 m2 g−1) compared with Pt/C (58.1 m2 g−1), which implies the presence of more catalytic sites in Fe@Pt/C. In addition, the Fe@Pt/C exhibits enhanced electron-conductivity properties, thus could afford faster ORR kinetics. Overall, all the results obtained in this study, including current density, mass activity, onset potential, and diffusion-limited current density, indicate that the Fe@Pt/C has remarkable electrocatalytic stability and activity compared with Pt/C.

Deming et al. [186] synthesized Pd/GQDs by thermolytic reduction of metal salts at 80 °C using GQDs followed by hydrothermal treatment at higher temperatures of 140, 160, 180, and 200 °C. The redox reaction activity was tested in an alkaline medium, and the results suggested a marked variation in the catalytic performance for the five samples. For example, the onset potentials for the as-prepared Pd/GQDs, Pd/GQD-140, Pd/GQD-160, Pd/GQD-180, and Pd/GQD-200 were found to be +0.88, +0.89, +0.86, +0.90, and +0.90 V, respectively. Similarly, the limiting potentials exhibited a remarkable variance among the Pd/GQD specimen, with the highest being 2.02 × 10−2 A/gPd for Pd/GQD-180. Thus, this indicates that the Pd/GQD-180 specimen possesses the lowest level of undesired peroxides species generated during OR, which makes it the best catalyst among other samples. In addition, Pd/GQD-180 exhibited the optimal level of transfer of electrons, mass activity, and specific activity. It also demonstrated the most favorable interactions between the Pd nanoparticles and the structural defects of GQDs, which resulted in the best binding interactions with oxygen intermediates on palladium surfaces. Hence, it could be concluded that the Pd/GQD-180 possesses the optimal ORR performance among the samples.

Vinoth et al. [187] synthesized N-GQDs decorated on 3D MoS2-rGO by the hydrothermal route. The resultant 3D nanohybrid showed a remarkable improvement in the electrocatalyst for the redox reaction with an alkaline medium. The electrocatalytic performance was observed by the enhanced onset potential of +0.81 V (vs. SHE) and by the superior CH3OH tolerance property of highly concentrated CH3OH (3 M). Furthermore, the nanohybrid exhibited improved ORR stability up to 3000 cycles with no obvious change in the half-wave potential. Therefore, these properties make the as-obtained nanohybrid a great candidate for selective cathode electrocatalyst in DMFCs applications. It is noteworthy to mention that the ORR catalytic performance relies heavily on the size of the synthesized N-doped GQDs. For instance, Zhang et al. [188] reported that different sizes of N-GQDs possessed varying catalytic activity. This study clearly shows the smallest N-GQDs have the lowest overpotential, which indicates the highest ORR catalytic activity. It was also noted that as N-GQDs’ size increased, the strength of adsorption of ORR intermediates, as well as the reaction-free energy of the rate-determining step, both increased. Similarly, N-GQDs/graphene hybrid was synthesized by Fan et al. [189] via two hydrothermal signs of progress to immobilize N-GQDs on graphene. The as-prepared N-GQDs/graphene demonstrated superb electrocatalytic performance for ORR due to the presence of the pyridinic-N. It also possessed stability for a long period, good conductivity, and high resistance to methanol crossover. Methanol crossover is a major problem in DMFCs, especially, when Nafion membranes are employed, which lowers the fuel cell potential and efficiency [190].

Jin et al. [191] synthesized a carbon-based oxygen reduction reaction catalyst by hybridizing GQDs with graphene nanoribbons (GNR) via a single-step co-reduction of methylbenzene and bromobenzene by sodium. The as-prepared GQDs-GNR catalyst displayed more positive onset potential and greater ORR diffusion current density compared to the commercially used Pt/C (40%) catalyst. As for the methanol crossover effect, it also possessed high durability and good tolerance. In general, GQD-GNR offers a new direction for developing a large-scale ORR electrocatalyst with excellent ORR performance. Similarly, Liu et al. [192] synthesized multidimensional N-GQDs hybrids with PL emission that are tunable by embedding a zero-dimension (0-D) GQDs particle onto the two-dimension (2-D) GQDs nanosheet surface. The as-prepared multidimensional hybrid exhibited superior performance toward ORR in an alkaline solution.

Xu et al. [193] developed a rationally designed strategy for the in-situ reduction and formation of GQDs with S-doped graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (s-g-C3N4) via a one-step hydrothermal process. The as-prepared s-g-C3N4@GQDs nanohybrid displayed notably enhanced catalytic activity for ORR in comparison to pristine s-g-C3N4 and GQDs, demonstrating great potential in the future for energy and conversion applications. Furthermore, Banerjee et al. [194] prepared GQDs doped with sulfur and S-linker. They concluded that the combination of S-linker along with nitrogen heteroatom resulted in an enhancement in the ORR activity for the GQDs with an efficiency similar to state of art platinum. Gong et al. [195] prepared cobalt-based QDs loaded with C3N4/rGO. For comparison with Pt-based catalysts, they prepared three different catalysts, namely Co3O4–C3N4, C3N4/rGO, and Co3O4–C3N4/rGO. The Co3O4–C3N4/rGO has an excellent ORR activity with an onset and half-wave potential comparable to Pt/C evident from Fig. 17 .

In another study, Yan et al. [196] prepared a catalyst for ORR on microbial fuel cells based on a combination of B-doped GQDs and Co/Fe-metal organic framework (BGQDs/MOF-t) were t refers to the time of deposition (t = 5, 15, 20 min).
