It is clear from Fig. 18 that their developed catalyst revealed a higher ORR activity compared to Pt/C based catalyst with a maximum power generation of 703.55 mW m−2. Furthermore, Lan et al. [197] allowed an in situ growth of nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon QDs (N, S-CQDs) on a Cu/foam (N, S-CQDs/Cu2O–Cu NWs). They concluded that N, S-CQDs/Cu2O–Cu NWs were capable of producing a power density 1.34 times that of Pt/C with a maximum amount of 925 mW m−2. Gou et al. [198] studied the effect of hydronium and hydroxide ion groups in nitrogen-doped GQDs. Their investigation showed that the best performance for the oxygen reduction reaction was achieved when H3O was absorbed on the gN-GQDs.

In an interesting study by Sun et al. [199], reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) was prepared using a thermal route as a supporting medium for nitrogen-doped graphene QDs (N-GQD). The TEM image reveals the uniform overlapping of the N-GQD granular on the r-GO, as shown in Fig. 19 (a) and (b). Their developed electrode benefited from the lower overpotential on the onset potential −0.1 V vs. SCE for N-GQDs/r-GO-250 compared to −0.2 V vs SCE for bare r-GO, as demonstrated in Fig. 19 (c). Furthermore, N-GQDs/r-GO-250 revealed a better four-electron transfer compared to bare reduced graphene oxide as is clear from Fig. 19 (d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots in fuel cells membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Malik et al. [200] constructed novel cross-linked membranes, based on polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and CdSe-QDs functionalized with carboxylic and sulfonic groups by ligand exchange method. Experimental results have shown that the functionalized QDs act as cross-linker, proton donors, and proton charge carriers. It was also found that all cross-linked membranes possess better thermal and hydrolytic stability compared to neat PVA membranes. Furthermore, a clear improvement in proton conductivity when both the temperature and amount of functionalized QDs were increased. Furthermore, Parthiban et al. [201] introduced Nafion-carbon QDs hybrid membrane with a remarkable decrease in the fuel crossover for the methanol fuel cell. Nafion-CQDs exhibit higher membrane stability in contrast to pristine Nafion membranes, as a result, the considerable interactions between the CQDs functional groups and the hydrophilic domain of Nafion membranes. It also possessed higher water uptake capacity (WUC); this could be a result of the hydrophilicity of the functional groups in CQDs, which will consequently enhance proton conductivity. The improved activity of Nafion-CQDs membrane with marked thermal and mechanical stability signals the possibility of using hybrid membranes in the application of DMFC. Similarly, Shukla et al. [202] reported the synthesis of sulfonated graphene nanoribbons-sulfonated GQDs (sGNR-sGQD) nanohybrid via a one-step treatment under ultrasonication in chlorosulfonic acid followed by a diazotization route. The unique structural characteristics of the hybrid allowed for better dispersion in sulfonated poly(ether ether ketone) (SPEEK) to form a nanocomposite membrane (1.5 wt%) electrolyte for DMFC. Compared with Nafion 117 and pristine SPEEK, the nanohybrid exhibited superior DMFC performance with a higher power density of around 40% as well as higher durability up to 100 h, as shown in Fig. 20 . This enhancement could be attributed to better physicochemical properties for the SPEEK/sGNR-sGQDs nanohybrid such as uptake of water, ion-exchange capacity, proton conductivity, and reduced methanol crossover, hence why it can be considered a great candidate for many membrane applications.

Furthermore, Pan et al. [203] adopted CQDs to improve the function of Pt catalysts. In this study, CQDs and platinum are comparable to a bi-component catalyst (i.e., PtRu). Compared with the commercially used PtRu/C catalyst (30% Pt + 15% Ru), the as-obtained Pt/XC72-CQDs5 displayed remarkable electrocatalytic activity and methanol electrooxidation reaction stability toward DMFC. The cyclic voltammetry test revealed a specific activity of 1.06 mA cm−2 Pt and 477.6 mA mg−1 Pt for Pt/XC72-CQDsS5, whereas that of PtRu/C was s 0.77 mA cm−2 Pt and 280.6 mA mg−1 Pt.

In addition, Wu et al. [204] introduced a molecular-level hybridization for Nafion with QDs as a new filler. Polymer-like QDs (PQDs), which are hydrophilic, and graphene oxide QDs, which are hydrophobic were synthesized via an easy and controllable approach with QD loading of 5%. The proton conductivity of the hybrid Nafion membrane exhibited a 124% increase compared to the commercial Nafion. Similarly, it displayed a 60% enhancement in hydrogen fuel cell output power while maintaining high mechanical stability. Moreover, there was a remarkable improvement in the conductivity of protons with relative humidity. For instance, at 20% RH, the conductivity of the hybrid Nafion was two times higher than the normal Nafion membranes. Similarly, the hybrid Nafion exhibited a maximum current density of 1.56 A cm−2 and a power density of 407 mW cm−2 at 60C and 75% RH. In contrast, the Nafion reached a maximum current density and power density of 1.05 A cm−2 and 255 mW cm−2, under the same conditions. This indicates excellent hydrogen cell performance. Furthermore, the graphene oxide QDs in the backbone nanophase decrease the crystalline nature and can remarkably enhance the swelling capacities of the membrane as well as its water uptake. The QDs applications in the anode and the cathode reactions are shown in Table 5 .

Previous literature shows that QDs reveal promising properties in fuel cells' applications as electrode materials. This could be ascribed to the presence of different functional groups (COOH, OH, and NH2) at their outer surface, where they greatly contribute to enhancing the stability and electrochemical activity in fuel cells’ applications [205]. For instance, CQD/M-rGO revealed better stability than Pt/C as an ORR electrocatalyst with only a 3% loss of the current density compared to a 15% loss in the current density after 10,000 cycles [181]. Table 5 provides a summary of these promising results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots in solar cell applications
ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy is seen as a potential way to satisfy the need for energy and the environmental crisis caused by conventional energy because it is highly abundant (sun) and it is totally clean. The amount of energy radiated from the sun is about 1.8 × 1011 MW and around 70% can be absorbed by the earth due to atmospheric scattering and absorptions [208].

Photovoltaic cells (solar cells) are electrical devices that convert light energy into electricity. Photovoltaic systems are more attractive when compared to other renewable energy technologies and that is because they are environmentally friendly with no crucial operational pollution with less operating and maintenance costs [209]. However, for high efficiency, solar cells need to absorb a range energy corresponding to the solar spectrum range (250–2500 nm) as shown in Fig. 21 [210]. In photovoltaic cells, QDs have gained scientists’ interest because of their desirable qualities: district energy level, size, energy tunability, long relaxation time due to the phonon bottleneck effect, and high-intensity light absorption and emission.

Sensitized solar cells are one of the most investigated third-generation photovoltaic cells and that is due to their low cost, ease of fabrication, and relatively high efficiencies [211]. Their structures comprise of a porous semiconducting thin film with adsorbed dye molecules, and they are inspired by photosynthesis, the natural process that plants use to convert sunlight into energy [212,213]. The semiconductor sensitized photovoltaic cells. Specifically, QDs sensitized solar cells (QDSCs), are rapidly growing QD solar cells. They show great development potential for future generations due to their special features, including their moisture, thermal, and light stability, ease of tunable absorption range, and coefficient [214–216]. Santra et al. [217] synthesized a QDSC with Mn-doped-CdS/CdSe plugged on mesoscopic titanium dioxide as a photoanode and Cu2S/Graphene oxide composite electrode. They prepared four types of semiconductors photoanode: (1) 10 SILAR cycles of undoped CdS, (2) 10 SILAR cycles of Mn-doped-CdS, (3) 5 cycles of undoped CdS followed by 8 cycles of CdSe (CdS/CdSe), and (4) 5 cycles of Mn-doped CdS followed by 8 cycles of CdSe (Mn-doped-CdS/CdSe). They reported that the best current density and conversion efficiency was achieved by Mn-doped-CdS/CdSe with a current density of 20.7 mA m−2 and 5.42%.

Santra et al. [218] prepared CulnS2 with a particle diameter of 2.6 nm, deposited on mesoscopic titanium dioxide. The current density reported was 8.9 mA/m2 and a power conversion efficiency of 1.14%. Wang et al. [219] synthesized liquid electrolyte-based QDSCs (L-QDSCs) using the same CdS/CdSe cosensitized photoanode. They used polysulfide electrolytes in the liquid state and Cu2S counter electrode on a brass plate. The L-QDSCs achieved 13.05 mA/m2 current density and a power conversion efficiency of 4.22%. Yu et al. [220] prepared CdSe QDSCs by in situ electrodeposition method. They concluded that the power conversion efficiency was at a mere 4.18%. Moreover, the current density and open-circuit voltages were 18.23 mA m−2 and 489 mV, respectively.

Ren et al. [221] enhanced the effectiveness of the sensitized electrode by coating it with a layer with a combination of blocking layers to curb charge recombination. They modified the TiO2 and the QDs with an amorphous layer (am-TiO2). The power conversion efficiency was as high as 9.28%, (JSC = 20.69 mA cm−2, VOC = 0.7 V). Du et al. [222] developed Zn–Cu–In–Se alloyed QD deposited on TiO2. They concluded that the current density was as high as 25.18 mA m−2 with a PCE of 11.66% and VOC 0.742 V. Tian et al. [223] studied the effect of methanol to remove the wettability and permeability of the electrodes in the TiO2 film. Two PbS-QDSC were studied, one without methanol and the other with 30% methanol. The PCE was increased from 2.41% to 4.01% when 30% CH3OH replaced the deionized water. Moreover, the current density was improved by a factor of 1.6 (11.68–18.37 mA cm−2). Hou et al. [224] fabricated a CdS/Mn–CdSe/ZnS QDs co-sensitized vertical ZnO nanorod arrays film (TiO2/CdS/CdSe:Mn/ZnS QD solar cell) to enhance the photovoltaic performance. They concluded that the current density was 12.6 mA cm−2, (PEC 4.14%, VOC = 0.74 V). Phuc et al. [225] prepared five different types of QDSCs (CdS/TiO2, CdSe/TiO2, ZnS/CdSe/CdS/TiO2, ZnS/CdSe:Cu/CdS/TiO2 and ZnS/CdSe: Mn/CdS/TiO2 QDSCs). The QDs sensitized solar cells based on doped films recorded the best results with the highest efficiency of 4.31% and a current density of 19.04 mA cm−2. Table 6 summarizes the characteristics of different types of QDSCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic and inorganic quantum dots solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: Sun et al. [240] achieved a higher photocurrent by the incorporation of Zn-doped CuInS2 (Zn–CIS) QDs into the QDs TiO2/PbS matrix. They achieved a power conversion efficiency of 7.74% with 0.34 V open-circuit voltage and a current density of 1.54 mA cm−2. Cao et al. [241] also synthesized PbS QD field effect transistors (FETs) on p-doped silicon substrates with a thermally grown oxide layer that has a thickness of 285 nm. The reported PCE was 9.6%, (JSC = 17.37 mA cm−2, VOC = 0.46 V). Davis et al. [242] synthesized monodisperse, single-crystal PbSe nanorods of superb quality via a one-pot reaction in the absence of a catalyst, using a novel phosphine selenide precursor, lead selenide (PbSe) nanorod solar cell. A 21 mA cm−2 current density was achieved with PCE of 2.52% and an open-circuit voltage of 0.29 V. Mali et al. [243] CH3NH3PbBr3 solid-state perovskite QD solar cell was fabricated using an ex-situ solution process for the synthesis of CH3NH3PbBr3. The reported open circuit voltage reached 1.11 V with a PCE of 11.40% and a current density of 14.07 mA cm−2.

Latif et al. [244] synthesized a novel QDs composed of TiO2-fMWCNTS nano-composite filled mesoporous anatase TiO2 in three devices 1, 2, and 3 that were sensitized with PbS by 10, 15, and 20 SILAR cycles, respectively. They concluded that the conversion efficiency increased as the successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) cycle increased. Hence, device 3, which is composed of Pbs QDs with 20 SILAR cycles, has achieved a power conversion efficiency of 5.6% compared to device 1 (10 SILAR cycles) and device 2 (15 SILAR cycles), where they have a 3.2% and 5% conversion efficiency, respectively. Moreover, the results of the AFM topographic image reveal that the mean particle size of PbS QDs is directly proportional to the SILAR cycle, therefore; increasing the cycles reveals a growth in the particle size. Finally, the highest efficiency of 5.6% was recorded with the greatest concentration of Pbs QDs, ascribed to the least bandgap of 2.2 eV and maximum charge combination resistance (Rcr) 2852 Ω.

Perez et al. [245] synthesized high-grade PbS colloidal QDs (CQDs) using the precipitation of negative and positive ions Pb+2 and S2−, respectively. The precipitation of ions was done on aqueous nanodroplets using ultrasonication to generate these ions. The novel PbS CQDs reveal a cubic crystal structure with a mean particle size of 6.8 nm, a size dispersion of 15%, and an optical band gap of 0.72 eV. Fig. 22 shows the best results for the current density versus voltage (J-V) of PbS-sensitized TiO2 solar cells after 15, 30, and 60 min of electrophoretic deposition. They concluded that the 60 min photoelectrode has the most power conversion efficiency of 0.72% compared to 0.54 and 0.22% for the 30 and 15 min, respectively.

Dissanayake et al. [246] studied the effect PbS QDs doped on polysulfide nanofiber gel polymer electrolyte on the titanium dioxide solar cells synthesized in CdS-QDs. They recorded that the efficiency of polysulfide liquid electrolyte-based CdS QD solar cells has improved from an initial value of 1.19–1.51% upon the addition of PbS-QDs (5% wt./wt.). A similar trend was seen for nanofiber gel electrolyte-based CdS QD solar cells, where the efficiency improved from 0.94 to 1.46%. This improvement in efficiency is because of the increase in sulfide ions (S2−), which caused a rise in the short circuit photocurrent density.

Liu et al. [247] observed the outcome of CdS/CdSe co-synthesized solar cells based on zinc titanium metal oxide with a layer of Zn/SiO2. Fig. 23 shows the HRTEM of MMOs/QDs coated with a double layer of Zn/SiO2. The effect of double passivation doubled layer of Zn/SiO2 revealed a curb in the charge recombination, higher electrons collection efficiency, and superior surface quality. Moreover, it showed an improvement in the power conversion efficiency with a percentage of 4.61%, which is 36% higher than that device using only ZnS passivation layer and more than 55% improvement compared to devices that do not use any passivation layer.

Ganguly and Nath [248] investigated the effect of Mn+2 doped CdS QDs as a sensitizing layer on ZnO thin-film solar cells. They concluded that a noticeable power conversion efficiency of 2.09% was recorded compared to pure CdS-QDs with a conversion of 1%. Moreover, they noticed that Mn+2 doped CdS-QDs on ZnO revealed the highest short circuit current density of 5.2 mA cm−2, an open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.6 V, and a fill factor (FF) of 0.67. Mnasri et al. [249] used CdS-QDs to improve the absorption of light and to increase PEC. They synthesized CdS-QDs solar cells with graphene oxide-titanium dioxide (GO-TiO2). Their studies showed an increase in power conversion efficiency from 0.09 to 0.3%. Furthermore, they analyzed the C–V characteristic of the PV cell under + ve and -ve voltage. They concluded that these measurements revealed different behavior. For instance, when tested in the voltage range −2 to +2 V, a positive capacitance at low frequency and a negative capacitance above 50 kHz were observed. This can be attributed to the fact of injection of electrons from the FTO electrode into TiO2.

Prastani et al. [250] prepared crystalline SnS and SnS/In2S3 core-shell QDs with a mean size of 4 nm for the application of photovoltaic devices. The conductivity mapping characterization of these QDs has been carried out by the means of Torsional Resonance Tunneling AFM (TR-TUNA). The measurements showed that the nanoparticles exhibit good electrical conductivity, thus making them suitable for solar cell applications along with other superior properties.

Shim et al. [251] studied the core (active material)/shell (conductive material) type Co3O4/QGDs and reported the in-situ chemical synthesis and their properties, which could be used as the electrode materials efficient electrochemical energy storages with the absence of conductive materials. Unlike the Co3O4, the Co3O4/GQDs electrode possessed a larger cyclic voltammogram (CV) area at a scan rate of 40 mV s−1, which indicates a higher level of stored charge because of the thin graphene shell, which could improve the electrical conductivity and chemical stability, prevent aggregation, and buffer the stress of the inner nanoscale active material [252]. Moreover, the conductivity of the Co3O4/GQDs electrodes was improved by building a stable bridge bond between stable oxygen and the oxygen functional groups on the graphene. This makes the electrode very promising for a promising electrochemical pseudo-capacitor application without using any conductive additives.

Seibert et al. [253] performed a thermal conductivity study for GQDs by revealing the thermal and mechanical properties of composites made by infusing GQDs into polymers at different mass loadings to the epoxy matrix (1, 2.5, and 5 wt%). It was observed that the thermal conductivity has increased linearly with the increase in mass loadings of GQDs and reached a maximum of 144% increase for 5 wt% loading of GQDs in epoxy.

Jiang et al. [254] synthesized modified GQDs with ZnCdS (ZnCdS/G) composites by using varying concentrations of the GQDs as follows 0.03, 0.06, and 0.12 wt%. The GQDs-modified ZnCdS (ZnCdS/G) was prepared by the solvothermal method to enhance the semiconductor properties. The results showed that when the GQDs were at 0.06 wt%, the electrochemical impedance was at its minimum. Furthermore, the highest photocurrent of 11.4 μA cm−2, which is tenfold greater than pure ZnCdS. The remarkable photoelectric and electrochemical properties were ascribed to the high conductivity of GQDs, which is capable of accelerating the photo-generated electron-hole pairs’ separation efficiency and inducing the reduction of the interfacial electronic resistance.

Chen et al. [255] claimed to have reported study one about the direct impregnation of GQDs with a mean size of 3.1 nm into a mesoporous porphyritic zirconium-based metal-organic framework (MOF). The results showed that the electrical conductivity of GQD-PCN-222 was at an average of 9 × 10−11 S cm−1, in the case where donor-acceptor charge transfer was present in the framework, which is 2 orders of magnitude greater than that of the PCN-222 pellets and similar to that of the GQD pellets. This is because the conduction band of GQDs is more negative than that of the porphyritic linkers in the MOFs, thus the donor-acceptor charge transfer was seen from the electron-donating GQDs to the electron-withdrawing linkers. Similarly, due to the enhanced electrical conductivity and high porosity of GQD-PCN-222, enhanced electrocatalytic activity was observed in contrast to the less conducting PCN-222 and nonporous GQDs. Moreover, the series and charge-transfer resistances of GQD-PCN-222 thin film were found to be 63 and 30 Ω, respectively, which are significantly smaller than the ones obtained for PCN-222 (105 and 190 Ω). These findings further suggest that the GQD-PCN-222 thin-film displays an improved conductivity, therefore, achieves a greater oxidation-reduction activity of its TCPP linkers in comparison to PCN-222 thin film.

A 5-nm-sized GQDs were used by Niu et al. [256] as a novel and efficient conductive agent and as an alternative for the common carbon black (CB) in the activated carbon (AC)-based supercapacitors. It was found that the specific capacitance and rate performance were drastically improved with GQDs at a suitable weight ratio of 1%in the electrode material, compared to that of the commercial CB with a weight ratio of 10%. This superior conductivity of GQDs is the result of the quantum confinement and edge effect [257]. Similarly, the composite electrode exhibited enhanced electronic conductivity and cycling stability in high-temperature environments due to the removal of oxygen functional groups. However, it is important to note that the absence of oxygen functional groups has negatively influenced the ionic conductivity as well as the rate performance.

Shadjou et al. [258] outlined the preparation of an electrochemical sensor based on GQDs and chitosan-modified glassy carbon electrode (GQD–CS–GCE) to detect vitamin C at low potentials. As a result of the superior electrical conductivity of GQDs, the peak currents significantly rose for the bare GQDs-GCE. However, adding active substances such as CS was essential to draw a comparison between the lower concentrations of vitamin C. Hence, GQD-CS was found to impart an acceleration center to the electron shuttling. This discovery is attributed to the good electrical conductivity and the inherent ability of GQD-CS. Furthermore, Qing et al. [259] introduced a simple GQs technique to enhance the performance of the AC for the application of advanced energy storage materials. Results indicated that the kinetics of activated carbon showed a remarkable improvement due to the conductivity network enhancement, which greatly enhances electrochemical kinetics (i.e., charge and ion transfer) in both deep and branched micropores.

Considerable attention has been given to lithium-based batteries owing to their popularity in many energy storage applications. Electrodes materials and charge transfer are considered a critical role in achieving better performance and higher efficiency [260,261]. In the context of using QDs as an anode for lithium-based batteries, Li et al. [262] successfully innovated a new anode composite (MnOQD@CHNTs) based on MnO QDs. In a typical synthesis, metal oxide QDs-carbon nanotubes are synthesized through the pyrolysis route, followed by the deposition of MnOQDs in the carbon layers to form MnOQD@CHNTs, as shown in Fig. 24 a-c. A cross-linked hetero-CNTs/QDs with a well-uniformed morphology of the developed catalyst, as illustrated in Fig. 24 d with open ends CNTs, as demonstrated in Fig. 24 e. Their developed composite took advantage of the high specific surface area (400.28 m2 g−1), revealing a large-area heterostructure. To study the effect of the developed composites, they prepared an electrode based on “MnOQD@CHNTs and Li foil” in coin-type 2025 cells. At a charge and discharge of 0.1 and 20 A g−1, respectively, the developed electrode revealed a reversible capacity of 452.8 mAh g−1 owing to their fast insertion of Li-ion into the MnOQD@CHNTs, as shown in Fig. 24 e. Furthermore, it revealed capacitance retention of up to 99% after 1000 cycles of charging and discharging at 20 and 0.1 A g−1, respectively. One of the most challenges facing Li-ion based batteries, that utilize composite polymer electrolytes with inorganic fillers, are the weak interaction between the filler and the polymer affecting the ionic conductivity. Bao et al. [263] incorporated ZnO QDs into poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) through the vapor phase infiltration method. They concluded that their developed electrode (VPI–ZnO/PEO/LiTFSI CSPE) revealed a discharged capacity of 164.7 mAh g−1 at 0.5C (1C = 200 mA g−1)) at 50 °C. Further enhancement for poor intrinsic conductivity of Sn-based oxide anodes was achieved using heterostructured QDs (SnO2@SnS2@NG) with a reversible charge capacity of 1081 mA h g−1 at 0.05 A g−1 [264]. In a different study involving potassium-ion batteries, Wu et al. [265] synthesized carbon nanosheets based on VN QDs as anode material via the thermal combustion route. They recorded a reversible capacity of 228 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1after 100 cycles. Table 7 summarizes the performance characteristics of QDs applications on batteries.

Despite the advances made in QDs research and in various applications, they still suffer from numerous limitations. The problem associated with their surface defects seems to have a major influence on the PL efficiency because it can affect the recombination of the electrons [274]. Furthermore, the toxicity of QDs is another challenge that dictates their application to the biomedical and biological fields. This is associated with the use of mercaptoacetic “cytotoxic” as a coating material for QDs [275]. In the context of energy storage applications, different surface modifications (i.e., salinization, mercaptopropionic acid, and polymer coating) have a negative impact on their efficiency, and they cannot be considered safe materials to handle. Finally, their high production cost and synthetic complexity need to be further addressed for large-scale production. The future trends in the use of QDs include energy storage applications, food packaging applications for a more sustainable path [276], and next-generation electroluminescent displays [277].

The QDs are characterized by their small and tunable size, high QY, constant composition, and fluorescence features that can advance the efficiency of many electrochemical applications. The use of different chemical and physical methods for preparing QDs, such as surface functionalization with polymers and different functional groups has received considerable research attention. Chemical modification via heteroatom doping, where the carbon atom in the GQDs is replaced by a heteroatom such as nitrogen, sulfur, or selenium. This is a well-recognized strategy for their unique architecture modification.

The stability of QDs can be enhanced by introducing an intermediate shell (core-shell structure) which is known to affect their performance positively. This paper showed that various studies revealed an enhancement in QDs’ stability, QY, and PL intensity under severe conditions. This review also reported the challenges of QDs synthesis and modifications. The main challenge is their stability. Hence, several approaches were practiced in that direction. For example, the direct growth of QDs in some applications, such as on the top of solar QDs nanocrystals, is still an issue. Furthermore, efficient optimization of their shell thickness by inserting alloyed core-shell structure directly reflects the QDs photochemical stability. Ligands have been classified as a class for stabilizing agents for QDs as they bind on the surface of the QDs, thus, enhancing their thermal stability as well as their photostability. Another method that received considerable attention for stabilizing QDs is polymer encapsulating of the QDs (overcoating) from oxygen and water.

Many studies reported the effect of QDs on enhancing the overall efficiency in different applications (i.e., sensors, photocatalysis, fuel cells, and solar cells). For example, the CsPbI3 perovskite QD solar cell revealed a superior efficiency enhancement of 15.21% [278]. Various other studies revealed that implementing QDs as a photocatalyst reveals a considerable enhancement that outweighs platinum group metals (PGM). Furthermore, QDs have made notable progress as electrocatalysts with a high positive potential with a lower onset potential. For instance, the implementation of CQDs to reduced graphene oxide (rGO/CQDs) will contribute to lowering the onset potential by more than >50 mV. This promising ORR activity improvement is attributed to the presence of electron-rich atoms in CQDs, affecting the electronegativity of the atoms while boosting the electron transfer [279]. Notably, the Nafion membrane suffers from reduced efficiency owing to methanol crossover; however, implementing filler-based CQDs in the Nafion membrane reduced the methanol crossover by 33% [201].

QDs possess excellent photocatalytic activity for water-splitting reactions due to their feasible surface functionalization, thus, affecting the energy band gap of the QDs. Owing to their superior electron transfer and proton conductivity, QDs showed remarkable performance in the anode, cathode, and membrane materials of fuel cells. Finally, their district energy levels, various sizes, and different energy tunability make them potential candidates in other photovoltaic cell applications. The research paradigm that involves synthesis, modification, and application must include more studies on robustness, cost-effectiveness, and toxicity before any implementation on a large scale for commercial applications. Future efforts should be directed toward the development of mature techniques for size control, morphology, and size distribution. Enhanced and well-established modification protocols are much needed either via chemical or core-shell heterostructures technique.

Future trends should focus on the synthesis routes of QDs and deeper understanding of their chemistry and gathering enough information for the synthetic reaction mechanism of high-quality and environmentally friendly QDs synthesis. Furthermore, the development of QDs at a large scale should be addressed as this will potentially alter the direction of different upcoming technologies owing to QDs different features. The commercialization of niche applications has the potential of great progress. However, the development of lower-cost synthesis routes and proper development techniques for its stability and tuning is still required.
